card.title: The Mariana Trench
card.teaser: If you dropped Mount Everest into the ocean's deepest scar, its peak would still be buried under a mile of water.
card.coverImagePrompt: A lone submersible suspended in absolute blackness, tiny floodlights catching marine snow drifting upward, the curve of a trench wall looming behind, cinematic, eerie, painterly

lessons.title: A wound in the planet
lessons.body: Off the coast of Guam, the Pacific seafloor suddenly caves in. The Mariana Trench plunges nearly 11,000 meters down — deeper than Everest is tall. Only three people have ever touched its floor. More humans have walked on the Moon.

visual.imagePrompt: A cross-section illustration comparing Everest's height to the depth of the Mariana Trench, to scale, moody color palette

lessons.title: Where two plates collide
lessons.body: The trench isn't just deep — it's a scar from a slow-motion collision. Here, the Pacific Plate is being shoved beneath the smaller Mariana Plate, a process called subduction. As one slab dives under the other, it warps the seafloor into a long, narrow canyon stretching over 2,500 kilometers.

keyTerms.definition: When one tectonic plate slides beneath another, often forming deep trenches
The deepest known point of the Mariana Trench, about 10,935 meters down

visual.imagePrompt: Geological cross-section of the Pacific Plate subducting under the Mariana Plate, glowing magma plumes, educational illustration

lessons.title: The weight of the world
lessons.body: At the bottom, pressure reaches 1,086 bar — the equivalent of 100 elephants standing on your head. Styrofoam cups sent down there come back shrunken to the size of thimbles. Yet somehow, life thrives: translucent snailfish, ghostly amphipods, and microbes that munch on hydrocarbons seeping from the rock.

visual.imagePrompt: A crushed styrofoam cup next to a full-sized one, dramatic studio lighting, scientific still life

lessons.title: The plastic at the bottom
lessons.body: When explorer Victor Vescovo reached the Challenger Deep in 2019, he found something nobody wanted to see: a plastic bag and candy wrappers. Studies of trench amphipods later revealed microplastics in nearly every specimen. The deepest place on Earth is already part of our waste stream.
lessons.funFact: The trench floor is so remote that sunlight has never reached it — but human-made chemicals have.

visual.imagePrompt: A lone plastic bag drifting near the seabed in pitch-black water, sub lights illuminating it, haunting, photorealistic

lessons.title: Closer than the Moon, farther than imagination
lessons.body: The Mariana Trench is a reminder that Earth's most alien frontier isn't in space — it's right beneath our ships. We've mapped less than a quarter of the deep ocean floor in detail. The next great discoveries may be down, not up.

quiz.question: Roughly how deep is the Challenger Deep?
quiz.explanation: The Challenger Deep sits at roughly 10,935 meters — deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

quiz.question: What geological process formed the Mariana Trench?
options: Subduction of one tectonic plate under another
quiz.explanation: The Pacific Plate is subducting beneath the Mariana Plate, bending the seafloor into a deep trench.

quiz.question: What surprising thing did explorers find at the trench's floor?
quiz.explanation: In 2019, Victor Vescovo documented plastic debris at the very bottom of the Challenger Deep.

sources.title: Discovery of the deepest fish in the ocean
Human pollution reaches the deepest ocean trenches
